title: End of module: Trigonometry I — Triangles, Radians and Trig Graphs

questions.text: In triangle $ABC$, $A = 50^\circ$, $C = 72^\circ$ and $a = 10\,\text{cm}$. Use the sine rule to find $c$, to 2 decimal places.
questions.explanation: By the sine rule, $\dfrac{c}{\sin C} = \dfrac{a}{\sin A}$, so $c = \dfrac{a \sin C}{\sin A} = \dfrac{10 \sin 72^\circ}{\sin 50^\circ} \approx \dfrac{10 \times 0.9511}{0.7660} \approx 12.42\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A triangle has $a = 7$, $b = 10$ and $A = 40^\circ$ (angle $A$ is opposite side $a$). What does this tell you about angle $B$?
choices: There is exactly one possible value of $B$
There are two possible values of $B$ (the ambiguous case)
questions.explanation: This is SSA (two sides and a non-included angle) — the ambiguous case. Solving $\sin B = \dfrac{b \sin A}{a} = \dfrac{10 \sin 40^\circ}{7} \approx 0.918$ gives $B_1 \approx 66.7^\circ$ and $B_2 = 180^\circ - 66.7^\circ = 113.3^\circ$. Since $A + B_2 = 153.3^\circ < 180^\circ$, both are valid, so there are two possible triangles.

questions.text: A triangle has sides $a = 6$, $b = 7$, $c = 9$. Use the cosine rule to find the size of the largest angle (opposite side $c$), in degrees to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: The largest angle is opposite the longest side, $c = 9$. $\cos C = \dfrac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab} = \dfrac{36 + 49 - 81}{2 \times 6 \times 7} = \dfrac{4}{84} \approx 0.0476$, so $C = \cos^{-1}(0.0476) \approx 87.3^\circ$.

questions.text: Two sides of a triangle are $a = 5$ and $b = 8$, with an included angle $C = 30^\circ$. Find the area of the triangle.
Which pair of given information can be solved directly using the cosine rule but NOT started with the sine rule?
choices: Two sides and the included angle (SAS)
Two angles and the side between them (ASA)
One side and its opposite angle plus another angle
questions.explanation: The sine rule needs a known opposite side/angle pair to get started. SAS (two sides and the angle between them) has no such pair available, so it requires the cosine rule; AAS and ASA give enough angle information to use the sine rule directly.

questions.text: A sector of a circle has radius $9\,\text{cm}$ and angle $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ radians at the centre. Find the exact arc length in terms of $\pi$, giving your answer as a multiple of $\pi$ (e.g. enter 3 for $3\pi$).
questions.explanation: $s = r\theta = 9 \times \dfrac{\pi}{3} = 3\pi\,\text{cm}$, so the coefficient of $\pi$ is $3$.

questions.text: A sector of a circle has radius $4\,\text{cm}$ and angle $2.5$ radians at the centre. Find its area.
What is the period of $y = \tan x$, and how does it compare with the period of $y = \sin x$?
choices: $\pi$, which is half the period of $\sin x$
$\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, a quarter of the period of $\sin x$
questions.explanation: $\tan x$ repeats every $\pi$ radians ($180^\circ$), whereas $\sin x$ and $\cos x$ repeat every $2\pi$ radians ($360^\circ$) — so the tangent graph's period is half that of sine.
From the $30$–$60$–$90$ triangle (sides $1$, $\sqrt{3}$, $2$), the side adjacent to $60^\circ$ has length $1$ and the hypotenuse is $2$, so $\cos 60^\circ = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
In the $45$–$45$–$90$ triangle both legs equal $1$, so $\tan 45^\circ = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$.

questions.text: At which values of $x$ (for $0 \le x \le 2\pi$) does the graph of $y = \tan x$ have vertical asymptotes?
questions.explanation: $\tan x = \dfrac{\sin x}{\cos x}$ is undefined wherever $\cos x = 0$, which on $[0, 2\pi]$ occurs at $x = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $x = \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$ — exactly where the tangent graph has vertical asymptotes.
